0: SCIENCE
1: <header>Life cycle assessment of pasture-based dairy production systems: Current and future performance </header>

Jonathan Herron, 1* Donal O'Brien,2 and Laurence Shalloo1 Teagasc, Livestock Systems Research Department, Animal & Grassland Research and Innovation Centre, Moorepark, Fermoy, Co. Cork, Ireland P61 P302 2Teagasc, Environment, Soils and Land Use Department, Crops Environment and Land Use Research Centre, Johnstown Castle, Wexford, Ireland Y35 TC97
2: <header>ABSTRACT </header>

To overcome the environmental challenges faced by the global agricultural sector while also ensuring economic viability, dairy farmers must improve the ef- ficiency of their systems. To improve system efficiency, the performance of an average production system must be determined, as it establishes a benchmark from which the efficacy of proposed management practices and mitigation strategies can be assessed. Identified man- agement practices and mitigation strategies can then be compiled to create ambitious but realistic targets for the sector to strive toward. Therefore the objective of this study was to calculate the environmental perfor- mance of an average spring-calving pasture-based dairy system and an ambitious target dairy system. Life cycle assessment (LCA) of 2 pasture-based dairy systems were conducted: (1) current average spring-calving pasture-based dairy system (current), and (2) a spring- calving pasture-based dairy system that has achieved key performance targets set by the most efficient dairy systems (target). An existing dairy LCA model was up- dated with country-specific emission factors, life cycle inventory data, and recommended methodologies. The environmental impact categories assessed were global warming potential, nonrenewable energy depletion, acidification potential, and eutrophication potential (marine and freshwater). Two functional units were used: per kilogram of fat- and protein-corrected milk (FPCM), and per hectare. To assess the effects of the model updates, the current dairy system was simulated twice, once with the previous version of the dairy LCA model, and second with the updated LCA model. The addition of country-specific emission factors, updated inventory data, and implementation of recommended methods has resulted in global warming potential and nonrenewable energy depletion being reduced by 10.4%

and 10.9%, respectively. The updates had negligible ef- fects on acidification and eutrophication potential. The inclusion of assumptions around carbon sequestration in grassland further reduced global warming potential by 16.4%. Moving from the current dairy system to the target dairy system was reported to reduce the environ- mental impact per kilogram of FPCM across all impact categories investigated. When expressed per hectare, transitioning toward the target dairy system reduced acidification, freshwater eutrophication, and nonrenew- able energy depletion by 2.0%, 8.8%, and 13.8%, re- spectively. In contrast, transitioning toward the target dairy system increased global warming per hectare and, to a lesser degree, marine eutrophication potential per hectare. The increase in global warming and marine eutrophication potential per hectare was attributed to the increase in stocking rate and subsequently milk production per hectare (9,950 VS. 14,100 kg of FPCM/ ha). This study demonstrates that the adoption of management practices that improve system efficiency will reduce the environmental impact per kilogram of FPCM and can contribute to the future sustainability of pasture-based dairy systems. However, improved system efficiency can be offset by the associated in- crease in productivity, highlighting the importance of reporting multiple environmental impact categories and functional units to prevent pollution swapping. New research and mitigation strategies will be required to improve the environmental sustainability of dairy systems beyond the target system in the future. Key words: life cycle assessment, milk production, dairy, pasture-based

4: <header>INTRODUCTION </header>

Dairy is one of the most common and valuable ag- ricultural commodities, with global milk production in 2019 reaching 852 million metric tons, valued at 387 billion dollars (10% of total global valued added of agricultural products; FAO, 2021, 2020). Milk is not only a local commodity, being produced and consumed

in nearly all countries worldwide, but it is also a global commodity with approximately 76.7 million metric tons traded globally in 2019 (9% of milk produced; FAO, 2020). Dairy products have historically been acknowl- edged as an excellent source of nutrition, providing pro- teins, minerals, vitamins, and fatty acids to consumers, which are regarded as important for a balanced diet. Recent insights into the contribution of dairy products to human health indicate that dairy products are ben- eficial for muscle building, lowering blood pressure, and preventing tooth decay, diabetes, cancer, and obesity (Tunick and Van Hekken, 2015; Thorning et al., 2016). In the coming decade the global population is expected to grow by 0.9% per annum, with growth concentrated in developing regions. It is expected that this popula- tion growth, coupled with income growth, will increase the demand for livestock-derived products, with world milk production projected to increase by 1.75% per an- num (OECD-FAO, 2021).

Ruminant production systems that produce milk (81% cattle, 15% buffalo milk, and 4% for goat, sheep, and camel milk combined) have been identified as a no- table source of anthropogenic greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, the main driver of global warming and cli- mate change, with dairy cattle contributing 11.6% of 2019 global Agriculture, Forestry and Other Land Use emissions (FAO, 2021). The contribution of dairy farm- ing to GHG emissions is particularly emphasized in Ire- land, with the ruminant-dominant agricultural sector contributing 35.4% of 2019 national GHG emissions, and responsible for 93.3% and 93.0% of methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) emissions, respectively (EPA, 2021a). Furthermore, ruminant systems such as dairy- ing have been linked to other environmental concerns such as acidifying pollutants, leaching and runoff of nu- trients to water, and the depletion of earth's resources (Famiglietti et al., 2019). The agricultural sector is therefore faced with a complex challenge in meeting the demand of the growing global population while concur- rently mitigating climate change and preserving and enhancing ecosystems and natural resources.

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is an internation- ally recognized methodology used to determine the environmental impact of all stages embodied in the life of a product, service, or process (ISO, 2006a,b). Agricultural research has adopted LCA to determine the environmental impact of different products (Fami- glietti et al., 2019) and production systems (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Ledgard et al., 2009; O'Brien et al., 2012), and to assess the effects of management practices (Schils et al., 2005; Chobtang et al., 2017; Herron et al., 2021). Agricultural LCA studies often take a holistic view of an agricultural system up to the

point in which the product leaves the farm gate, where all processes that occur on farm and those associated with the production of farm inputs are included. The purpose of this system boundary is to determine the environmental impact of the farm stage of the life cycle of milk and to identify potential mitigation strategies that could be adopted on farm. Although international standards for LCA exist (ISO, 2006a,b), there are limi- tations with the methodology, largely associated with inconsistent application of the approach across studies. Regardless, LCA is considered the most robust and comprehensive approach to determine environmental impacts and evaluate potential mitigation strategies (Crosson et al., 2011). Life cycle assessment is continu- ously evolving through the adoption of new research results and recommended methodologies. It is therefore vital that LCA models are appropriately maintained and up to date with the most recent research and rec- ommended methodologies, to ensure that the models reflect the most recent and relevant research for a coun- try or region.

To overcome the environmental challenge faced by the global agricultural sector while also ensuring economic viability, dairy, along with other agricultural production systems, must improve the efficiency of their systems (Holden, 2020). This can be achieved through system change, technical progress, and the adoption of mitiga- tion strategies. However, it is important to be conscious that improving efficiency can increase productivity and subsequently the environmental impact of the produc- tion system. To realize the potential of said mitigation strategies, it is vital to determine the performance of an average production system. This sets a benchmark to which efficiency improvements can be compared. Based on the adoption of a range of strategies, an ambitious but realistic target production system can be estab- lished.

The objectives of this study were 2-fold: to update an existing dairy LCA model with country-specific emis- sion factors and recommended methodologies; and to calculate the environmental performance of an average Irish pasture-based dairy system and an ambitious tar- get pasture-based dairy system.

Although international standards for LCA exist (ISO, 2006a,b), there are limi- tations with the methodology, largely associated with inconsistent application of the approach across studies. Regardless, LCA is considered the most robust and comprehensive approach to determine environmental impacts and evaluate potential mitigation strategies (Crosson et al., 2011). Life cycle assessment is continu- ously evolving through the adoption of new research results and recommended methodologies. It is therefore vital that LCA models are appropriately maintained and up to date with the most recent research and rec- ommended methodologies, to ensure that the models reflect the most recent and relevant research for a coun- try or region.

To overcome the environmental challenge faced by the global agricultural sector while also ensuring economic viability, dairy, along with other agricultural production systems, must improve the efficiency of their systems (Holden, 2020). This can be achieved through system change, technical progress, and the adoption of mitiga- tion strategies. However, it is important to be conscious that improving efficiency can increase productivity and subsequently the environmental impact of the produc- tion system. To realize the potential of said mitigation strategies, it is vital to determine the performance of an average production system. This sets a benchmark to which efficiency improvements can be compared. Based on the adoption of a range of strategies, an ambitious but realistic target production system can be estab- lished.

The objectives of this study were 2-fold: to update an existing dairy LCA model with country-specific emis- sion factors and recommended methodologies; and to calculate the environmental performance of an average Irish pasture-based dairy system and an ambitious tar- get pasture-based dairy system.
10: <header>Life Cycle Assessment </header>

Goals and Scope. The goal of this study was to benchmark the environmental impacts and resource use of the current average dairy system in Ireland and to establish targets for each environmental impact indicator investigated, using performance targets and

best management practices. To achieve this goal, 2 pro- duction systems were simulated: the current national average pasture-based dairy system (current) and a pasture-based dairy system achieving high animal and farm performances currently being achieved by the most efficient pasture-based dairy farms (target).

The environmental impact categories reported were global warming, acidification, eutrophication, and nonrenewable energy depletion. A cradle-to-farm gate system boundary was adopted, encompassing on-farm process and those embodied in the production of farm inputs. Environmental burdens embodied in capital (e.g., farm infrastructure and machinery) and medicines were excluded from the LCA. The temporal coverage of the LCA model was one production year. The function- al units chosen were 1 kg of fat- and protein-corrected milk (FPCM; IDF, 2015) and 1 ha of on-farm area. Physical allocation (IDF, 2015) was used to disaggre- gate the environmental burden between milk and meat produced by surplus calves and cull cows.

Life Cycle Inventory. Life cycle inventory analy- sis was conducted using the LCA model described in Herron et al. (2021). Animal performance, forage production, and manure management data were used to populate the model. The model assumes that the production systems are constant (i.e., no expansion or contraction of herd size). Monthly feed intakes were calculated based on the total net energy requirements for milk production, maintenance, activity, pregnancy, and body weight change, and the net energy value per kilogram of DM (unité fourragère lait [UFL]/kg of DM) of each feed in a given month (O'Mara, 1996).

In recent years there have been advancements in LCA methodology, most notably in GHG emissions through the development of country-specific methodologies and continued refinements to international methodologies. To assess the effects of these advancements, this study assessed the environmental performance of the current average dairy system (current) using 2 methodologies: methodology 1 (M1), which predominantly used IPCC (2006) emission factors and historical factors; and methodology 2 (M2), which used the most recent IPCC (2019) guidelines and country- or regional-specific emission factors where possible. (Table 1). The target system was simulated using the M2 methodology only.

(Table 1). The target system was simulated using the M2 methodology only.
12: <header>Methane </header>

Methane from enteric fermentation during grazing was calculated using a fixed gross energy intake con- version factor (Ym EPA, 2021a). Gross energy intake is calculated based on DMI, calculated using the net energy system and the gross energy content (MJ/kg of DM) of each feed for a given month. A Y of 6.5%

of gross energy intake (IPCC, 2006) was used for M1, and a Ym of 6.3% was used for M2 (IPCC, 2019; Table 1). The country-specific equation develop by Yan et al. (2000) was used to calculate CH4 from enteric fermen- tation when cattle were housed and on a grass silage- based diet. Methane emissions from managed manure in M1 were calculated using the IPCC (2006) method- ology based on the quantity of volatile solids excreted during housing. Manure volatile solids excretion was calculated as the difference between total OM intake and digestible OM intake (O'Mara, 2006). The M2 used IPCC (2019) tier 2 methodology, which requires vola- tile solids excretion, monthly ambient air temperature, and manure removal information to calculate the CH4 conversion factor (Table 1).
13: <header>Nitrogen Emissions </header>

Nitrogen intake was calculated based on dry mat- ter intake and the crude protein content of animals' diet within each month. Nitrogen excretion rate was calculated in accordance with the national inventory methodology (EPA, 2021a). In M1 we used the IPCC (2006) tier 1 emission factor of 2% per kilogram of N applied to calculate N2O emissions from manure ex- creted during grazing. In contrast, M2 quantified N2O from this source using country-specific emission factors of 0.31% and 1.18% for manure N and urine N, respec- tively (Krol et al., 2016). Nitrous oxide emissions from synthetic N fertilizer application in M1 was calculated using the IPCC (2006) tier 1 emission factor of 1% N applied. In M2, country-specific emission factors were used to calculate N2O emissions from the application of urea-based and calcium ammonium nitrate fertilizer for M2 (Harty et al., 2016). The N mass flow approach was used to calculate N emissions from manure for M1 and M2 (Webb and Misselbrook, 2004). As adopted in the national informative inventory (EPA, 2021b), emission factors reported in the United Kingdom's inventory of ammonia (NH3) from agriculture (Misselbrook et al., 2016) and the EMEP/EEA (2019) guidebook were used to calculate NH3, nitrogen, and nitrogen oxide emissions from manure. Nitrous oxide emissions from manure storage and application were calculated using default IPCC (2019) emission factors for M1. Nitrous oxide emissions from manure application was calcu- lated using country-specific emission factors reported by Bourdin et al. (2014) in M2.
14: <header>Nutrient Leaching and Runoff </header>
According to the national GHG inventory report, 10% of N applied to agricultural soils is susceptible to nitrate (NO3) leaching. This emission factor was applied
in M1 and M2 (Table 1). The methodology developed by Nemecek and Kägi (2007) was used to calculate the quantity of phosphorus (P) applied to agricultural land that is lost through runoff.
15: <header>Carbon Dioxide </header>

Greenhouse gas emissions from the combustion of fos- sil fuel was calculated using emission factors reported in the Irish national inventory report (EPA, 2021a). As recommended in the most recent IPCC (2019) guide- lines, emission factors of 0.12 kg of CO2-C per kilogram of lime were used to calculate CO2 emissions from the

decomposition of lime on agricultural soils. Similarly, an emission factor of 0.20 kg of CO2-C per kilogram of urea was used to calculate CO2 emissions from urea hydrolysis (IPCC, 2019). Several studies have reported permanent pastures actively sequestering C and act- ing as a C sink (Jaksic et al., 2006; Soussana et al., 2010). Byrne et al. (2018) reported net C sequestration rates of -2 to 1 C/ha per year, depending on soil type, soil pH, and management practices. To account for C sequestration, global warming potential (GWP) was reported with and without C sequestration. An average Irish C sequestration rate of 0.5 t of C/ha per year was used (Byrne et al., 2018).

20: Greenhouse gas emissions and nonrenewable energy depletion from the combustion of fossil fuels on farm were calculated based on data and emission factors reported in the Irish national inventory report (EPA, 2021a). Similarly, GHG emissions and nonrenewable energy depletion embodied in electricity were calculat- ed using emission factors reported in the Irish national inventory report (EPA, 2021a) along with energy flow data provided by SEAI (2020). As used in previous version of the LCA model, GHG emissions and non- renewable energy depletion from fertilizer production were calculated using emission factors provided by Ecoinvent (2010), Snyder et al. (2009), and Kramer et al. (1999), based on 1990s technology (M1). Through the adoption of abatement technologies, N2O emissions from nitric acid production have reduced by 80 to 90% compared with pre-abatement emission levels (Brent- rup et al., 2016). Furthermore, the energy efficiency of fertilizer production has improved over time. In re- sponse to these progressions, Hoxha and Christensen (2019) developed up-to-date emission factors for the main fertilizer products across different regions (Table 1). These new emission factors were used for M2. All remaining pollutants of concern from these activities were sourced from SimaPro (PRé Sustainability, 2021). Information on the environmental burden associated with the production of imported feeds was sourced from the Global Feed LCA Institute (GFLI, 2021). Carbon dioxide loss from land use change was included. Economic allocation was applied. Life cycle inventory data for herbicides, plastic, transportation of feed, and production of fossil fuels were sourced from SimaPro (PRé Sustainability, 2021).
21: To interpret the results of the life cycle inventory, emissions and resources were transformed into midpoint environmental impacts using characterization factors. The 100-yr time horizon characterization factors (with- out climate change feedback) reported by IPCC (2013) were used to calculate GWP. The country-specific char- acterization factors (moles of H+ equivalent) reported by Posch et al. (2008) were used to calculate acidifica- tion potential (AP). The ReCiPe 2016 v1.1 method- ology (Huijbregts et al., 2016) was used to calculate eutrophication, where the methodology disaggregates eutrophication into marine (MEP, kg of N equivalent) and freshwater eutrophication potential (FEP, kg of P equivalent) based on the limiting nutrients. Nonre- newable energy depletion (NRE) was calculated as the
quantity of finite fossil fuels consumed during on- and off-farm activities, expressed in megajoules.
22: Current Dairy System. The current farm scenario involved simulation of the environmental performance of the national average dairy system for the period 2017 to 2019 (Table 2). The dairy systems simulated were spring-calving, pasture-based systems, as this is the predominant system in Ireland. Animal inventory data were sourced from the Central Statistics Office (CSO, 2021a). The populations of all livestock in Ireland are recorded biannually (June and December). This is a highly reliable data source, which is used to meet EU requirements regulation (EC) no. 1165/2008, concern- ing livestock and meat statistics. Average herd calving and fertility performance (e.g., 6-wk calving rate and replacement rate) was obtained from the Irish Cattle Breeding Federation's annual dairy calving statistics report (ICBF, 2021). The Irish Cattle Breeding Federa- tion's national database contains genetic, phenotypic, and genomic data that is used to provide information services to the agri-food industry through genetic gain. The database is populated by farmers and other indus- try partners (e.g., milk recording organizations, meat factories). National milk delivery data were obtained from the Central Statistics Office (CSO, 2021b). The statistical population of the Central Statistics Office database includes all creameries and pasteurizers in Ireland, where its purpose is to provide information on the quantity of milk processed by dairy processors and therefore the quantity of milk delivered by farm- ers. This is a highly reliable data source, as it used for EU requirements laid out in directive 96/16/EC. Data for on-farm management practices such as manure management, concentrate feed supplementation rate, fertilizer levels applied, and housing or turnout dates were obtained from Teagasc's National Farm Survey (Donnellan et al., 2020). This survey is operated as part of the EU Farm Accountancy Data Network, where it takes a random, nationally representative sample of the national population of farms and records physical (e.g., farm inputs, practices) and economic (e.g., sales, income) information.

Target Dairy System. The key performance in- dicators for the target system are outlined in Table 2. These key performance indicators were based on the animal and grassland targets currently being achieved by the most efficient pasture-based dairy farms. The system included key performance indicators in the ar- eas of dairy cow fertility, milk production, grassland management, and on-farm management practices, with
an aim to improve the economic performance of dairy systems while simultaneously reducing the associated environmental burden.

Increasing the 6-wk calving rate from 62% to the 90% industry target and calving COWS earlier in spring allows for a more consistent herd that matches energy requirement to pasture grass growth, thus maximizing homegrown fresh forage in their diet. In addition to a compact calving period, the implementation of a breed- ing strategy that improves fertility and survivability maintains a compact calving, reduces the number of nonproductive replacement animals, and optimizes milk production per hectare. The 90% 6-wk calving rate and the 18% replacement rate were established as industry targets by ICBF (2021) based on the current performance of top herds. Along with fertility and sur- vivability, implementing breeding strategies that select for milk solids (fat and protein content) increases the nutritional and economical value of the milk produced. Since the introduction of the economic breeding index, milk fat and protein have been increasing at rates of 0.025% and 0.015% per annum, respectively (CSO, 2021b). Through the implementation of breeding strat- legies, the target system therefore increased the milk fat and protein content from 4.11% and 3.49% to 4.61% and 3.70%, respectively.

Improving grassland management in the target sys- tem increased pasture growth and utilization, thus al- lowing the system to sustain higher stocking rates (2.1 VS. 2.7 livestock units per hectare), increase production per hectare, and improve nutrient use efficiency. The increase in pasture production in the target system was based on the average annual yield achieved by farm- ers using the Teagasc PastureBase decision support tool, representing 3,250 dairy farms (Hanrahan et al., 2017; Teagasc, 2021a). The subsequent target stocking rate of 2.7 livestock units per hectare is currently be- ing achieved by the top 20% (categorized by net profit per hectare) of a nationally representative subsample of dairy farms (Hanrahan et al., 2019). PastureBase reduced reliance on imported concentrate feed (1,026 kg of DM/cow VS. 450 kg of DM/cow) because of improved forage production and quality. The target system increased soil amendment (i.e., lime) and ad- dressed deficiencies in soil phosphorus and potassium indexes to optimize soil fertility. White clover was in- corporated in swards to reduce the system's synthetic N fertilizer requirement while also increasing forage yields (185 kg of N/ha VS. 150 kg of N/ha). Forage protein content was subsequently increased from 18% to 19%. Unlike the current dairy system, which applied 87% of N using NO3-based fertilizers, the target system

has adopted low emission technologies through 100% use of protected urea. Additionally, the target system applied 100% of slurry using trailing shoe technology, as opposed to the traditional splash plate method used in the current system. Converting to trailing shoe tech- nology increases the fertilizer value of slurry applied in spring and summer months by 29% to 60%, respectively (Teagasc, 2021b).

This survey is operated as part of the EU Farm Accountancy Data Network, where it takes a random, nationally representative sample of the national population of farms and records physical (e.g., farm inputs, practices) and economic (e.g., sales, income) information.

Target Dairy System. The key performance in- dicators for the target system are outlined in Table 2. These key performance indicators were based on the animal and grassland targets currently being achieved by the most efficient pasture-based dairy farms. The system included key performance indicators in the ar- eas of dairy cow fertility, milk production, grassland management, and on-farm management practices, with
an aim to improve the economic performance of dairy systems while simultaneously reducing the associated environmental burden.

Increasing the 6-wk calving rate from 62% to the 90% industry target and calving COWS earlier in spring allows for a more consistent herd that matches energy requirement to pasture grass growth, thus maximizing homegrown fresh forage in their diet. In addition to a compact calving period, the implementation of a breed- ing strategy that improves fertility and survivability maintains a compact calving, reduces the number of nonproductive replacement animals, and optimizes milk production per hectare. The 90% 6-wk calving rate and the 18% replacement rate were established as industry targets by ICBF (2021) based on the current performance of top herds. Along with fertility and sur- vivability, implementing breeding strategies that select for milk solids (fat and protein content) increases the nutritional and economical value of the milk produced. Since the introduction of the economic breeding index, milk fat and protein have been increasing at rates of 0.025% and 0.015% per annum, respectively (CSO, 2021b). Through the implementation of breeding strat- legies, the target system therefore increased the milk fat and protein content from 4.11% and 3.49% to 4.61% and 3.70%, respectively.

Improving grassland management in the target sys- tem increased pasture growth and utilization, thus al- lowing the system to sustain higher stocking rates (2.1 VS. 2.7 livestock units per hectare), increase production per hectare, and improve nutrient use efficiency. The increase in pasture production in the target system was based on the average annual yield achieved by farm- ers using the Teagasc PastureBase decision support tool, representing 3,250 dairy farms (Hanrahan et al., 2017; Teagasc, 2021a). The subsequent target stocking rate of 2.7 livestock units per hectare is currently be- ing achieved by the top 20% (categorized by net profit per hectare) of a nationally representative subsample of dairy farms (Hanrahan et al., 2019). PastureBase reduced reliance on imported concentrate feed (1,026 kg of DM/cow VS. 450 kg of DM/cow) because of improved forage production and quality. The target system increased soil amendment (i.e., lime) and ad- dressed deficiencies in soil phosphorus and potassium indexes to optimize soil fertility. White clover was in- corporated in swards to reduce the system's synthetic N fertilizer requirement while also increasing forage yields (185 kg of N/ha VS. 150 kg of N/ha). Forage protein content was subsequently increased from 18% to 19%. Unlike the current dairy system, which applied 87% of N using NO3-based fertilizers, the target system

26: Current Dairy System. The current farm scenario involved simulation of the environmental performance of the national average dairy system for the period 2017 to 2019 (Table 2). The dairy systems simulated were spring-calving, pasture-based systems, as this is the predominant system in Ireland. Animal inventory data were sourced from the Central Statistics Office (CSO, 2021a). The populations of all livestock in Ireland are recorded biannually (June and December). This is a highly reliable data source, which is used to meet EU requirements regulation (EC) no. 1165/2008, concern- ing livestock and meat statistics. Average herd calving and fertility performance (e.g., 6-wk calving rate and replacement rate) was obtained from the Irish Cattle Breeding Federation's annual dairy calving statistics report (ICBF, 2021). The Irish Cattle Breeding Federa- tion's national database contains genetic, phenotypic, and genomic data that is used to provide information services to the agri-food industry through genetic gain. The database is populated by farmers and other indus- try partners (e.g., milk recording organizations, meat factories). National milk delivery data were obtained from the Central Statistics Office (CSO, 2021b). The statistical population of the Central Statistics Office database includes all creameries and pasteurizers in Ireland, where its purpose is to provide information on the quantity of milk processed by dairy processors and therefore the quantity of milk delivered by farm- ers. This is a highly reliable data source, as it used for EU requirements laid out in directive 96/16/EC. Data for on-farm management practices such as manure management, concentrate feed supplementation rate, fertilizer levels applied, and housing or turnout dates were obtained from Teagasc's National Farm Survey (Donnellan et al., 2020). This survey is operated as part of the EU Farm Accountancy Data Network, where it takes a random, nationally representative sample of the national population of farms and records physical (e.g., farm inputs, practices) and economic (e.g., sales, income) information.

Average herd calving and fertility performance (e.g., 6-wk calving rate and replacement rate) was obtained from the Irish Cattle Breeding Federation's annual dairy calving statistics report (ICBF, 2021). The Irish Cattle Breeding Federa- tion's national database contains genetic, phenotypic, and genomic data that is used to provide information services to the agri-food industry through genetic gain. The database is populated by farmers and other indus- try partners (e.g., milk recording organizations, meat factories). National milk delivery data were obtained from the Central Statistics Office (CSO, 2021b). The statistical population of the Central Statistics Office database includes all creameries and pasteurizers in Ireland, where its purpose is to provide information on the quantity of milk processed by dairy processors and therefore the quantity of milk delivered by farm- ers. This is a highly reliable data source, as it used for EU requirements laid out in directive 96/16/EC. Data for on-farm management practices such as manure management, concentrate feed supplementation rate, fertilizer levels applied, and housing or turnout dates were obtained from Teagasc's National Farm Survey (Donnellan et al., 2020). This survey is operated as part of the EU Farm Accountancy Data Network, where it takes a random, nationally representative sample of the national population of farms and records physical (e.g., farm inputs, practices) and economic (e.g., sales, income) information.

Target Dairy System. The key performance in- dicators for the target system are outlined in Table 2. These key performance indicators were based on the animal and grassland targets currently being achieved by the most efficient pasture-based dairy farms. The system included key performance indicators in the ar- eas of dairy cow fertility, milk production, grassland management, and on-farm management practices, with
an aim to improve the economic performance of dairy systems while simultaneously reducing the associated environmental burden.

Increasing the 6-wk calving rate from 62% to the 90% industry target and calving COWS earlier in spring allows for a more consistent herd that matches energy requirement to pasture grass growth, thus maximizing homegrown fresh forage in their diet. In addition to a compact calving period, the implementation of a breed- ing strategy that improves fertility and survivability maintains a compact calving, reduces the number of nonproductive replacement animals, and optimizes milk production per hectare. The 90% 6-wk calving rate and the 18% replacement rate were established as industry targets by ICBF (2021) based on the current performance of top herds. Along with fertility and sur- vivability, implementing breeding strategies that select for milk solids (fat and protein content) increases the nutritional and economical value of the milk produced. Since the introduction of the economic breeding index, milk fat and protein have been increasing at rates of 0.025% and 0.015% per annum, respectively (CSO, 2021b). Through the implementation of breeding strat- legies, the target system therefore increased the milk fat and protein content from 4.11% and 3.49% to 4.61% and 3.70%, respectively.

Improving grassland management in the target sys- tem increased pasture growth and utilization, thus al- lowing the system to sustain higher stocking rates (2.1 VS. 2.7 livestock units per hectare), increase production per hectare, and improve nutrient use efficiency. The increase in pasture production in the target system was based on the average annual yield achieved by farm- ers using the Teagasc PastureBase decision support tool, representing 3,250 dairy farms (Hanrahan et al., 2017; Teagasc, 2021a). The subsequent target stocking rate of 2.7 livestock units per hectare is currently be- ing achieved by the top 20% (categorized by net profit per hectare) of a nationally representative subsample of dairy farms (Hanrahan et al., 2019). PastureBase reduced reliance on imported concentrate feed (1,026 kg of DM/cow VS. 450 kg of DM/cow) because of improved forage production and quality. The target system increased soil amendment (i.e., lime) and ad- dressed deficiencies in soil phosphorus and potassium indexes to optimize soil fertility. White clover was in- corporated in swards to reduce the system's synthetic N fertilizer requirement while also increasing forage yields (185 kg of N/ha VS. 150 kg of N/ha). Forage protein content was subsequently increased from 18% to 19%. Unlike the current dairy system, which applied 87% of N using NO3-based fertilizers, the target system

has adopted low emission technologies through 100% use of protected urea. Additionally, the target system applied 100% of slurry using trailing shoe technology, as opposed to the traditional splash plate method used in the current system. Converting to trailing shoe tech- nology increases the fertilizer value of slurry applied in spring and summer months by 29% to 60%, respectively (Teagasc, 2021b).
30: It is recommended in ISO (2006b) to evaluate the sen- sitivity of the life cycle inventory and life cycle impact assessment by assessing the assumptions and method- ologies used in an LCA when interpreting results and drawing conclusions and recommendations. This study has conducted a sensitivity analysis on the effect of allocation method to ensure that results are correctly interpreted. The allocation methods evaluated were (1) economic (baseline), (2) mass-based, (3) protein-based, and (4) physical causality, using equations reported in the IDF (2015) LCA guidelines.

The target system has implemented management practices and available technologies to achieve the key performance indicator targets. A sensitivity analysis was conducted to determine the effects of individual system changes on environmental performance. The target key performance indicators assessed for each independent scenario are outlined in Table 2. Previous research (O'Neill et al., 2011; Jiao et al., 2014; Hynes et al., 2016) and recent data (B. Lahart, Teagasc, Cork, Ireland, personal communication; M. Kennedy, Teagasc, Cork, Ireland, personal communication) have reported lower Ym from grazing dairy cattle than the recommended IPCC (2019) default conversion factors. It is possible that using the IPCC (2019) default Ym will overestimate enteric CH4 emissions from grazing systems. A sensitivity analysis investigating the effect of using region-specific Ym for grazing dairy cattle and during the housing period on grass silage was therefore
conducted. Methane conversion factors from grazing studies and recent data were compiled to calculate con- version factors (Appendix Table A1).

0: <title>RESULTS </title>
1: <header>Global Warming Potential </header>

The results of the impact assessments are reported in Table 3. In the current system, the updated LCA model (M2) reported lower GWP per kilogram of FPCM [0.97 VS. 1.08 kg of carbon dioxide equivalents (CO2-eq) kg of FPCM] and per hectare (9,633 VS. 10,743 kg of CO2-eq/ha) than the previous version of the LCA model (M1). The reduction in GWP for the current dairy system was attributed to reduced N2O emissions from manure excreted at pasture, synthetic fertilizer application, and synthetic fertilizer production.

The results of the target system were only compared with results of the current dairy system with M2 meth- odology, as the target dairy system was simulated us- ing the most up-to-date methodology (M2). The GWP of the target dairy system was 0.21 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM less than the current dairy system, due to improved efficiencies and the adoption of available technologies. When expressed per hectare, the target system emitted 11% or 1,056 kg of CO2-eq more per hectare than the current average dairy system. The rise in emission intensity per hectare was due to a 42% in- crease in productivity per hectare (9,950 VS. 14,150 kg of FPCM/ha), enabled by improved grassland manage- ment and fertility performance; that is, the increase in production per hectare was proportionally greater than the reduction in GWP per kilogram of FPCM.

Methane emissions were the dominant GHG, contrib- uting 60.3% and 69.0% of total GHG emissions from the updated current and target dairy systems, respec- tively (Table 4). Enteric fermentation was the dominant source of CH4, emitting 0.55 kg of CO2-eq and 0.50 kg of CO2-eq per kilogram of FPCM from the current

and target dairy systems, respectively. The quantity of CH4 emitted per kilogram of FPCM in the target dairy system was lower than the current dairy system due to higher FPCM yield and improved fertility performance (Table 4). The target dairy system emitted more CH4 per hectare than the current dairy system due to higher stocking rate (7,121 VS. 5,427 kg of CO2-eq/ha).

Nitrous oxide was the second highest source of GHG emissions, contributing 27.4% and 22.0% of total GWP for the current dairy system using M1 and M2, respec- tively (Table 4). When M1 was applied, manure excreted at pasture was the main source of N2O from the current dairy system, followed by fertilizer application and pro- duction. In contrast, when M2 was applied, synthetic fertilizer application was the dominant source of N2O (0.09 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM), followed by manure excreted at pasture and concentrate feed production. The reduction in N fertilizer rate and the transition from calcium ammonium nitrate-based fertilizers to protected urea fertilizers in the target system decreased direct N2O emission from fertilizer application by 82%. As a result, manure excreted at pasture was the main source of N2O emissions from the target system (0.05 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM). Other key N2O sources in- cluded synthetic fertilizer application, concentrate feed production, and manure management. The adoption of low-emission technologies and improved efficiencies in the target system had a particular mitigating effect on N2O, reducing total N2O by 27.3% (465 kg of CO2-eq/ ha).

Carbon dioxide represented the lowest share of the 3 main GHG emissions, emitting 17.7% (0.17 kg of CO2- eq/kg of FPCM) and 15.5% (0.12 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM) of total GHG emissions from the current and target systems, respectively (Table 4). The reduction in concentrate supplementation and synthetic fertilizer application rate reduced CO2 per kilogram of FPCM by 32%. Due to increased productivity per hectare, no differences in CO2 per hectare were reported for the current and target dairy systems. The inclusion of grassland carbon sequestration offset the GWP by 1,835 kg of CO2-eq/ha, and 0.16 and 0.11 kg of CO2-eq/ kg of FPCM for the current average and target dairy systems, respectively.

The results of the impact assessments are reported in Table 3. In the current system, the updated LCA model (M2) reported lower GWP per kilogram of FPCM [0.97 VS. 1.08 kg of carbon dioxide equivalents (CO2-eq) kg of FPCM] and per hectare (9,633 VS. 10,743 kg of CO2-eq/ha) than the previous version of the LCA model (M1). The reduction in GWP for the current dairy system was attributed to reduced N2O emissions from manure excreted at pasture, synthetic fertilizer application, and synthetic fertilizer production.

The results of the target system were only compared with results of the current dairy system with M2 meth- odology, as the target dairy system was simulated us- ing the most up-to-date methodology (M2). The GWP of the target dairy system was 0.21 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM less than the current dairy system, due to improved efficiencies and the adoption of available technologies. When expressed per hectare, the target system emitted 11% or 1,056 kg of CO2-eq more per hectare than the current average dairy system. The rise in emission intensity per hectare was due to a 42% in- crease in productivity per hectare (9,950 VS. 14,150 kg of FPCM/ha), enabled by improved grassland manage- ment and fertility performance; that is, the increase in production per hectare was proportionally greater than the reduction in GWP per kilogram of FPCM.

Methane emissions were the dominant GHG, contrib- uting 60.3% and 69.0% of total GHG emissions from the updated current and target dairy systems, respec- tively (Table 4). Nitrous oxide was the second highest source of GHG emissions, contributing 27.4% and 22.0% of total GWP for the current dairy system using M1 and M2, respec- tively (Table 4). Other key N2O sources in- cluded synthetic fertilizer application, concentrate feed production, and manure management. The adoption of low-emission technologies and improved efficiencies in the target system had a particular mitigating effect on N2O, reducing total N2O by 27.3% (465 kg of CO2-eq/ ha).

The inclusion of grassland carbon sequestration offset the GWP by 1,835 kg of CO2-eq/ha, and 0.16 and 0.11 kg of CO2-eq/ kg of FPCM for the current average and target dairy systems, respectively.

and target dairy systems, respectively. The quantity of CH4 emitted per kilogram of FPCM in the target dairy system was lower than the current dairy system due to higher FPCM yield and improved fertility performance (Table 4). The target dairy system emitted more CH4 per hectare than the current dairy system due to higher stocking rate (7,121 VS. 5,427 kg of CO2-eq/ha).

Nitrous oxide was the second highest source of GHG emissions, contributing 27.4% and 22.0% of total GWP for the current dairy system using M1 and M2, respec- tively (Table 4). When M1 was applied, manure excreted at pasture was the main source of N2O from the current dairy system, followed by fertilizer application and pro- duction. In contrast, when M2 was applied, synthetic fertilizer application was the dominant source of N2O (0.09 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM), followed by manure excreted at pasture and concentrate feed production. The reduction in N fertilizer rate and the transition from calcium ammonium nitrate-based fertilizers to protected urea fertilizers in the target system decreased direct N2O emission from fertilizer application by 82%. As a result, manure excreted at pasture was the main source of N2O emissions from the target system (0.05 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM). Other key N2O sources in- cluded synthetic fertilizer application, concentrate feed production, and manure management. The adoption of low-emission technologies and improved efficiencies in the target system had a particular mitigating effect on N2O, reducing total N2O by 27.3% (465 kg of CO2-eq/ ha).

Carbon dioxide represented the lowest share of the 3 main GHG emissions, emitting 17.7% (0.17 kg of CO2- eq/kg of FPCM) and 15.5% (0.12 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM) of total GHG emissions from the current and target systems, respectively (Table 4). The reduction in concentrate supplementation and synthetic fertilizer application rate reduced CO2 per kilogram of FPCM by 32%. Due to increased productivity per hectare, no differences in CO2 per hectare were reported for the current and target dairy systems. The inclusion of grassland carbon sequestration offset the GWP by 1,835 kg of CO2-eq/ha, and 0.16 and 0.11 kg of CO2-eq/ kg of FPCM for the current average and target dairy systems, respectively.
9: <header>Acidification Potential </header>

The updated LCA model (M2) calculated marginally lower AP for the current dairy system (6.8 E-03 molo H+-eq/kg of FPCM) than the previous version (M1; 7.0 E-03 molc H+-eq/kg of FPCM) due to new NH3 emis- sion factors for manure management (Table 1). The target dairy system emitted 31% and 2% less acidifying pollutants per kilogram of FPCM and per hectare than the current dairy system, respectively (Table 3).

Ammonia was the dominant acidifying pollutant, contributing 86% and 84% of total AP caused by the current and target systems, respectively (Table 5). The target dairy system emitted less NH3 per ton of FPCM and per hectare (3.9 E-03 molc H+-eq/kg of FPCM and

55.6 mol H+-eq/ha) than the current dairy system (5.9 E-03 mol H+-eq/kg of FPCM and 58.8 mol H+-eq/ha). Manure management (housing, storage, and applica- tion) were the main sources of NH3 emissions, releasing 3.5 and 2.6 mol H+-eq/kg of FPCM from current and target systems, respectively. Although extended graz- ing in the target system increased NH3 emitted during grazing, NH3 emissions from manure management re- duced due to the reduction in N excreted indoors. The adoption of low-emission slurry-spreading technology further reduced NH3 emissions from manure applica- tion. Lower fertilizer rates and the substitution of urea- based fertilizer with protected urea reduced total NH3 emissions from fertilizer application by 75% (4.7 mol H+-eq/ha). Concentrate feed production was the main off-farm source of NH3 releasing 1.1 E-03 and 5.7 E-04 molc H+-eq/kg of FPCM from the current and target systems. Following NH3, sulfur dioxide (SO2) was the second highest acidifying pollutant, contributing 11% and 14% of total AP for the current and target systems, respectively (Table 5). Electricity production was the main source of SO2 (92%) for both systems.

11: <header>Eutrophication Potential </header>

The update to the LCA model had negligible effect on MEP, as the majority of refinements were associated with GHG and NH3 emissions. Similar to GWP and AP, the target dairy system emitted 27% less MEP/ kg of FPCM than the current dairy system (Table 3). However, as also reported in GWP, the target system emitted 3.5% (0.7 kg of N-eq/ha) more MEP per hect- are than the current average dairy system.

Nitrate accounted for 73% and 74% of the MEP caused by the current and target dairy systems, respectively (Table 5). Synthetic fertilizer application and manure excreted during grazing were the dominant sources of NO3 to water for the current dairy system, releasing 5.1 and 3.7 kg of N-eq/ha, respectively. In contrast, manure excreted at pasture was the main NO3 source (5.9 kg of N-eq/ha) in the target system. This occurred due to higher stocking rate, longer grazing season, and lower synthetic N fertilizer application rate than the current system. Despite higher stocking rates, the target dairy system released similar MEP pollutants from manure management compared with the current system, due to a shorter housing period. Concentrate feed was the dominant off-farm source of MEP pollutants, emitting 3.2 and 2.3 kg of N-eq/ha from the current and target systems, respectively. The remaining MEP was largely attributed to NH3 emissions, where the same sources discussed above in AP apply.
The updates made to the LCA model had no effect on FEP (Table 3). Concentrate production (0.22 kg

of P-eq/ha) and manure excreted (0.11 kg of P-eq/ ha) at pasture were the dominant sources of FEP pol- lutants from the current dairy system (Table 5). The target system reported lower FEP per hectare and per kilogram of FPCM than the current system, despite higher stocking rate increasing FEP from manure ex- creted during grazing. The reduction was attributed to the reduction in concentrate feed supplementation and the increase in productivity per hectare in the target system.

14: <header>Nonrenewable Energy Depletion </header>

The updated LCA model (M2) calculated 10.8% lower NRE for the current dairy system than the previ- ous M1 version (2.53 VS. 2.84 MJ/kg of FPCM; Table 3). The reduction in NRE was attributed to less fossil fuels consumed in the production of synthetic fertil- izer and greater proportion of electricity generated by renewable energy sources. The target system reduced NRE consumer per kilogram of FPCM and per hectare by 39% (1.0 MJ/kg of FPCM) and 13.5% (3,391 MJ/ ha), respectively (Table 3). The reduction in NRE con- sumption was attained through the reduced reliance on synthetic fertilizers and concentrate feed.

Over 90% of NRE consumed occurred off farm during the production of farm inputs (Table 5). On-farm use of fossil fuels in the operation of machinery was the sole on-farm activity that consumed NRE. Concentrate feed production (11,648 MJ/ha) was the largest consumer of NRE in the current dairy system, followed by fertil- izer production (8,560 MJ/ha). The reduction in con- centrate feed supplementation and synthetic fertilizer application in the target system reduced the associated NRE use per hectare by 29% and 10%, respectively. Nonrenewable energy consumed during the generation of electricity was a minor consumer of NRE for both systems. The target system consumed less NRE per kilogram of FPCM and per hectare than the current system due to greater productivity per hectare and reduced reliance on concentrate feed and fertilizer use.

Figure 1. Allocation factors for fat- and protein-corrected milk and meat produced by cull COWS and surplus calves for an average dairy system in Ireland and a target system using economic, mass-based (kg), physical (phy.) causality (IDF, 2015), and protein-based (kg) allocation methods.

a greater proportion of environmental burden to milk compared with the physical causality method. The results of the second sensitivity analysis inves- tigating the effect of independently achieving the key performance indicators are reported in Table 7. Increas- ing milk constituent content while maintaining milk yield had negligible effect on environmental impact per hectare due to increase in intake requirement per cow and thus reduction in stocking rate. Environmental im- pact per kilogram of FPCM reduced with the increase in milk constituent content. Improving the fertility performance of the herd by reducing replacement rate and improving the 6-wk calving rate reduced GWP, AP, and MEP per kilogram of FPCM by 1.7%, 4.0%, and 1.2% respectively. Switching all synthetic nitrogen fertilizer to protected urea and maintaining application rate reduced GWP, AP, and MEP (per hectare and per kilogram of FPCM) by 6.5%, 6.9%, and 2.6%, respec- tively. A 2.4% increase in NRE was reported. Reducing manure storage time by applying slurry earlier in the year and using low-emission slurry technology reduced GWP, AP, and MEP by 1.1%, 2.5%, and 1.0%, respec- tively. Reducing concentrate feed supplementation had a notable reduction across impact categories per hectare -11.3% to 27%) and per kilogram of FPCM (-2.4% to 19.7%). Improving grassland management and soil fertility, and incorporating white clover into swards increased environmental impact per hectare by 24% to 31% for MEP and GWP, respectively. When expressed per kilogram of FPCM, environmental impact per kilo- gram of FPCM reduced across categories investigated by 8.7% to 15.3% for GWP and MEP, respectively.

Figure 2 depicts the results of the third sensitivity analysis. The GWP intensity of the current and target systems reduced by 0.06 and 0.055 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM when Ym reported by grazing dairy stud- ies was used. Total enteric CH4 emissions reduced by 10.8% and 11.9% for the current and target systems, respectively. The GHG intensity per hectare reduced by 582 and 586 kg of CO2-eq for the current and target systems, respectively.

Improving the fertility performance of the herd by reducing replacement rate and improving the 6-wk calving rate reduced GWP, AP, and MEP per kilogram of FPCM by 1.7%, 4.0%, and 1.2% respectively. Switching all synthetic nitrogen fertilizer to protected urea and maintaining application rate reduced GWP, AP, and MEP (per hectare and per kilogram of FPCM) by 6.5%, 6.9%, and 2.6%, respec- tively. A 2.4% increase in NRE was reported. Reducing manure storage time by applying slurry earlier in the year and using low-emission slurry technology reduced GWP, AP, and MEP by 1.1%, 2.5%, and 1.0%, respec- tively. Reducing concentrate feed supplementation had a notable reduction across impact categories per hectare -11.3% to 27%) and per kilogram of FPCM (-2.4% to 19.7%). Improving grassland management and soil fertility, and incorporating white clover into swards increased environmental impact per hectare by 24% to 31% for MEP and GWP, respectively. When expressed per kilogram of FPCM, environmental impact per kilo- gram of FPCM reduced across categories investigated by 8.7% to 15.3% for GWP and MEP, respectively.

Figure 2 depicts the results of the third sensitivity analysis. The GWP intensity of the current and target systems reduced by 0.06 and 0.055 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM when Ym reported by grazing dairy stud- ies was used. Total enteric CH4 emissions reduced by 10.8% and 11.9% for the current and target systems, respectively. The GHG intensity per hectare reduced by 582 and 586 kg of CO2-eq for the current and target systems, respectively.
3: <header>DISCUSSION </header>

To meet the growing demand for agricultural com- modities such as dairy in a manner that is economically viable and environmentally sustainable, current agri- cultural production systems must improve their effi- ciencies and adopt new technologies and recommended practices. The GHG and NH3 abatement potential and the associated costs and benefits of available efficiency and technical mitigation measures in the agricultural sector have been reported in a national marginal abate- ment cost curves (Lanigan et al., 2018; Buckley et al., 2020). Using LCA, this study simulated the environ- mental impact of the current average spring-calving pasture-based dairy system with the Moorepark LCA model (O'Brien et al., 2012) before and after it was updated with the latest country-specific emission fac- tors and recommended methodologies.

The results of this study are consistent with the findings of Chobtang et al. (2017), who identified the increase in animal productivity and pasture utilization efficiency as promising intensification practices for

0: <title>Herron et al.: EFFICIENCY OF PASTURE-BASED PRODUCTION </title>

the New Zealand dairy sector from an environmental sustainability perspective. Increasing productivity per hectare by increasing farm inputs (i.e., synthetic fertil- izer and concentrate feed) was not deemed a sustainable intensification practice. The grassland management key performance indicator targets outlined in Table 1 and applied in the target system are currently being achieved on the very best commercial farms (Hanrahan et al., 2019; Teagasc, 2021a). It is important to note that not all dairy systems may be able to achieve the target pasture production on the same farm inputs or achieve the target grazing days, due to natural con- straints. Nevertheless, Hanrahan et al. (2019) generally showed that the management effect is greater than the physical constraint effect.

Solely reporting a product-based functional unit (i.e., kg of FPCM) can potentially result in an unbalanced view of the mitigation potential of a management practice (Salou et al., 2017). Although improving the efficiency of a production system reduces the environ- mental burden embodied in a product, it also creates scope for increased production levels and therefore potential increase in total environmental impact. As all production systems were simulated using the same farm size (i.e., 40 ha), a reduction per hectare therefore indicated a reduction in total environmental impact. Expressing environmental impact using an area-based functional unit is also more applicable for local environ- mental impacts such as eutrophication and acidifica- tion in determining the effects of management practices on surrounding ecosystems (de Vries et al., 2015). The transition toward the target system reduced or had negligible effects on AP, FEP, and NRE per hectare; however, the paradoxical consequence of improved effi- ciency occurred for GWP and, to a lesser degree, MEP. The sensitivity analysis identified the increased pasture production and subsequently higher stocking rates (i.e., more cows) to be the main cause of the Jevons paradox occurring. It is important to note, however, that the target system was associated with a 42% increase in milk output and only an 11% increase in emissions.

2: <header>Life Cycle Assessment Comparison </header>

Life cycle assessment is considered as the most ro- bust means of calculating the environmental impact of agricultural systems; however, despite the existence of international standards (ISO, 2006a,b), it is widely known that variations and inconsistencies in LCA methodologies occur, hindering meaningful compari- son of LCA studies (Crosson et al., 2011). To address this shortcoming of the LCA methodology, the current study conducted a sensitivity analysis on the effects of 4 allocation methods. Furthermore, the environmental

0: <title>Herron et al.: EFFICIENCY OF PASTURE-BASED PRODUCTION </title>
impact of production systems were scaled to 1 kg of the IDF-recommended fat- and protein-corrected milk metric and 1 ha (IDF, 2015).

Agricultural LCA often investigate a limited number of environmental impact categories for ease of interpre- tation, with 60% of cattle LCA studies reporting 1 to 3 impact categories (McClelland et al., 2018). Focusing on a singular impact category or a limited number of impact categories increases the risk of environmental trade-offs not being accounted for and thus could lead to conclusions that reduce one form of emissions while increasing another kind. To address this issue, the cur- rent study has included a range of impact categories to provide a more holistic view of the environmental impact of the dairy systems.

2: <header>Global Warming Potential </header>

Climate change, using GWP 100, is the most com- monly reported environmental impact category in livestock LCA studies (98% of studies reviewed; Mc- Clelland et al., 2018). Using the previous version of the dairy LCA model (M1; O'Brien et al., 2015) yielded an average GWP for grass-based Irish dairy farms. From a nationally representative sample of 221 farms, a GWP of 1.2 (0.60-2.13) kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM was reported. This range is caused by variation in key farm parameters such as stocking rate, milk produc- tion, grazing season length, synthetic fertilizer use, and concentrate feeding rate. O'Brien et al. (2015) high- lighted that increasing grazing season length and milk production per hectare were the main farm factors to target to reduce GHG intensity while also improving economic viability of systems. The manner in which the increase in milk production is achieved is pivotal, where minimizing reliance on farm inputs (i.e., concen- trate feed, fertilizer) should be targeted. The average of O'Brien et al. (2015) is comparable to a GWP of 1.12 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM for the current system using the old model (M1) when the same allocation method and GWP characterization factors were applied. When grassland C sequestration was included, O'Brien et al. (2015) reported a 12% reduction in GWP per kilogram of FPCM. In contrast, the current study reported a 15.1% and 16.4% reduction in GWP for the current system when M1 and M2 were applied, respectively. Although differences in system performance between studies partially explains the disparity, it is largely associated with different grassland C sequestration rates. Research is needed to improve our current un- derstanding of C sequestration to determine the effects of management practices and eventually establish an appropriate methodology to calculate C sequestration at the individual farm level.

Figure 2. Global warming potential (GWP) of 1 kg of protein-corrected milk (FPCM) and 1 ha for the current and target systems using the IPCC (2019) default enteric methane conversion factor and grazing-based methane conversion factor. CO2-eq = carbon dioxide equiva- lents; MCF = methane conversion factor; IPCC = Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change.

In recent years substantial research has improved our understanding of the key GHG emission sources from agriculture. For example, Krol et al. (2016) report im- proved N2O emission factors from manure excreted at pasture by disaggregating manure into urine and dung, and assessing different soil types, weather conditions, and seasonal effects. Similarly, Harty et al. (2016) has improved N2O emission factors from fertilizer applica- tion by disaggregating N fertilizer into fertilizer catego- ries, and assessing effects of soil type and climatic con- ditions on emissions. These new emission factors have been recently incorporated into the national inventory report and were applied in the M2 methodology in this study. It is important to note that such developments are improvements in understanding and not improve- ments in the production systems. This distinction is relevant, as improvements in understanding and thus methodology cannot be credited against any GHG re- duction targets. Improvements in technologies, such as the reduction in N2O emissions during fertilizer produc- tion and an increased proportion of electricity produced by renewable sources can be included as part of overall emissions reductions. Therefore, although adoption of the IPCC (2019) methodology and country- or region- specific emission factors reduced the GWP of the cur- rent dairy system (M1 VS. M2), the reduction was not achieved through on-farm mitigation.
The target dairy system represents what can be achieved through the adoption of recommended best practice at farm level. The reduction in environmental

intensity per kilogram of FPCM in the target system was achieved through the adoption of low-emission technologies, genetic gain, improved soil fertility and grassland management, and incorporation of white clover into the sward as recommended by Lanigan et al. (2018). Although GWP per hectare increased in the target system, if the stated mitigation strategies are widely implemented, milk output could increase by over 30% and result in no increase in emissions from the dairy sector

In New Zealand, Ledgard et al. (2020) calculated an average GHG intensity of 0.78 (0.75-0.81) kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM for the period from 2010 to 2018. Along with temporal variation, the authors reported a spatial variation with average GHG intensities from the years 2017 to 2018 varying from 0.72 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM in Otago to 0.86 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM in Southland. Chobtang et al. (2016) reported a similar average GHG intensity of 0.8 (0.78-0.82) kg of CO2-eq/ kg of FPCM from 52 farms in the Waikato region in New Zealand. These GHG intensities are notably lower than the results calculated in the current study. First, the LCA model used in the study by Ledgard et al. (2020) and Chobtang et al. (2016) uses country-specific emission factors; therefore we are comparing their re- sults to the results produced by the updated model in the current study (M2). Second, it is important to note that the GHG intensities reported by Ledgard et al. (2020) do not account for direct emissions from land use change, despite the fact that 70% of land conversion

from forestry was to dairying (pasture). If direct land use change is included, Ledgard et al. (2020) estimated that the average GHG intensity of New Zealand milk for the years 2017 to 2018 increased by 0.13 kg of CO2- eq/kg of FPCM to 0.91 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM.

Using the Integrated Farm System Model, Rotz et al. (2021) calculated an average GHG intensity of 1.01 (0.69-1.45) kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM produced across 6 regions and 5 farming systems in the United States. Although Rotz et al. (2021) included grazing systems, they accounted for only 16% of farms assessed, with confined dairy systems being the dominant production systems (75% of farms assessed). Similar to the cur- rent study, Rotz et al. (2021), Ledgard et al. (2020), and Chobtang et al. (2016) used the IPCC (2013) fifth assessment report GWP characterization factors (without climate change feedback) and adopted the IDF (2015)-recommended physical causality allocation method.

Although studies do not apply identical methodology, they all arrive at the same conclusion that enteric fer- mentation was the dominant source of GWP pollutants when using GWP 100 metrics, particularly pasture- based systems. Compared with the IPCC (2019) Ym of 6.3% (21.0 g of CH4/kg of DM) for dairy cattle, O'Neill et al. (2011) reported 5.7% (18.1 g of CH4/ kg of DM) during early mid-lactation, and Jiao et al. (2014) reported 5.3 to 5.9% (17.7-20.0 g of CH4/kg of DM) during mid-late lactation when using the SF6 technique. Hynes et al. (2016) reported a similar Ym of 5.6% (18.3 g of CH4/kg of DM) using the chamber method during mid-late lactation. Wims et al. (2010) and O'Neill et al. (2012) reported Ym of 6.4% and 7.8% during mid-lactation and late lactation, increasing with pre-grazing herbage mass and grass allowance, respec- tively. The elevated Ym could be attributed to grass quality; however, it also could be attributed to inherent variation in the SF6 technique, such as the overesti- mation of CH4 from the prolonged deployment of SF6 tubes (Deighton et al., 2013). These studies indicate that a seasonal effect, as observed by Robertson and Waghorn (2002), could be occurring. A similar trend was identified in recent research that determined the methane profile of grazing lactating dairy cattle over a full grazing season through the use of the GreenFeed technology (B. Lahart, Teagasc, Cork, Ireland, per- sonal communication). This technology has also been deployed for dry dairy cattle that were housed and of- fered a grass silage diet (M. Kennedy, Teagasc, Cork, Ireland, personal communication). It is envisaged that this novel research, along with previously published data, will be used to develop a tier 3 methodology for enteric CH4 emissions from dairy cattle. Based on the sensitivity analysis, GWP could reduce to 0.91 and 0.71
kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM for the current and target dairy systems, respectively.

Allen et al. (2018), Cain et al. (2019), and Shine (2009) highlighted a serious limitation of the GWP metric, in that it does not adequately differentiate the different behaviors of short- and long-lived climate pollutants and their effects on global mean surface temperature. The ambiguous relationship between the ubiquitous GWP metric and temperature response is concerning, particularly in regards to the Paris Agree- ment, which has a clear temperature-oriented target. To address this, Cain et al. (2019) have presented a new metric, GWP*, or CO2 warming equivalent, which treats an increase in the emission rate of short-lived climate pollutants as a one-off pulse emission rather than as cumulative emissions, and provides a clear link between GHG emissions and temperature response (Lynch et al., 2020). This metric is not suitable for inclusion in farm-based carbon footprints, due to data requirements.

Figure 2. Global warming potential (GWP) of 1 kg of protein-corrected milk (FPCM) and 1 ha for the current and target systems using the IPCC (2019) default enteric methane conversion factor and grazing-based methane conversion factor. CO2-eq = carbon dioxide equiva- lents; MCF = methane conversion factor; IPCC = Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change.

In recent years substantial research has improved our understanding of the key GHG emission sources from agriculture. For example, Krol et al. (2016) report im- proved N2O emission factors from manure excreted at pasture by disaggregating manure into urine and dung, and assessing different soil types, weather conditions, and seasonal effects. Similarly, Harty et al. (2016) has improved N2O emission factors from fertilizer applica- tion by disaggregating N fertilizer into fertilizer catego- ries, and assessing effects of soil type and climatic con- ditions on emissions. These new emission factors have been recently incorporated into the national inventory report and were applied in the M2 methodology in this study. It is important to note that such developments are improvements in understanding and not improve- ments in the production systems. This distinction is relevant, as improvements in understanding and thus methodology cannot be credited against any GHG re- duction targets. Improvements in technologies, such as the reduction in N2O emissions during fertilizer produc- tion and an increased proportion of electricity produced by renewable sources can be included as part of overall emissions reductions. Therefore, although adoption of the IPCC (2019) methodology and country- or region- specific emission factors reduced the GWP of the cur- rent dairy system (M1 VS. M2), the reduction was not achieved through on-farm mitigation.
The target dairy system represents what can be achieved through the adoption of recommended best practice at farm level. The reduction in environmental

intensity per kilogram of FPCM in the target system was achieved through the adoption of low-emission technologies, genetic gain, improved soil fertility and grassland management, and incorporation of white clover into the sward as recommended by Lanigan et al. (2018). Although GWP per hectare increased in the target system, if the stated mitigation strategies are widely implemented, milk output could increase by over 30% and result in no increase in emissions from the dairy sector

In New Zealand, Ledgard et al. (2020) calculated an average GHG intensity of 0.78 (0.75-0.81) kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM for the period from 2010 to 2018. Along with temporal variation, the authors reported a spatial variation with average GHG intensities from the years 2017 to 2018 varying from 0.72 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM in Otago to 0.86 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM in Southland. Chobtang et al. (2016) reported a similar average GHG intensity of 0.8 (0.78-0.82) kg of CO2-eq/ kg of FPCM from 52 farms in the Waikato region in New Zealand. These GHG intensities are notably lower than the results calculated in the current study. First, the LCA model used in the study by Ledgard et al. (2020) and Chobtang et al. (2016) uses country-specific emission factors; therefore we are comparing their re- sults to the results produced by the updated model in the current study (M2). Second, it is important to note that the GHG intensities reported by Ledgard et al. (2020) do not account for direct emissions from land use change, despite the fact that 70% of land conversion

from forestry was to dairying (pasture). If direct land use change is included, Ledgard et al. (2020) estimated that the average GHG intensity of New Zealand milk for the years 2017 to 2018 increased by 0.13 kg of CO2- eq/kg of FPCM to 0.91 kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM.

Using the Integrated Farm System Model, Rotz et al. (2021) calculated an average GHG intensity of 1.01 (0.69-1.45) kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM produced across 6 regions and 5 farming systems in the United States. Although Rotz et al. (2021) included grazing systems, they accounted for only 16% of farms assessed, with confined dairy systems being the dominant production systems (75% of farms assessed). Similar to the cur- rent study, Rotz et al. (2021), Ledgard et al. (2020), and Chobtang et al. (2016) used the IPCC (2013) fifth assessment report GWP characterization factors (without climate change feedback) and adopted the IDF (2015)-recommended physical causality allocation method.

Although studies do not apply identical methodology, they all arrive at the same conclusion that enteric fer- mentation was the dominant source of GWP pollutants when using GWP 100 metrics, particularly pasture- based systems. Compared with the IPCC (2019) Ym of 6.3% (21.0 g of CH4/kg of DM) for dairy cattle, O'Neill et al. (2011) reported 5.7% (18.1 g of CH4/ kg of DM) during early mid-lactation, and Jiao et al. (2014) reported 5.3 to 5.9% (17.7-20.0 g of CH4/kg of DM) during mid-late lactation when using the SF6 technique. Hynes et al. (2016) reported a similar Ym of 5.6% (18.3 g of CH4/kg of DM) using the chamber method during mid-late lactation. Wims et al. (2010) and O'Neill et al. (2012) reported Ym of 6.4% and 7.8% during mid-lactation and late lactation, increasing with pre-grazing herbage mass and grass allowance, respec- tively. The elevated Ym could be attributed to grass quality; however, it also could be attributed to inherent variation in the SF6 technique, such as the overesti- mation of CH4 from the prolonged deployment of SF6 tubes (Deighton et al., 2013). These studies indicate that a seasonal effect, as observed by Robertson and Waghorn (2002), could be occurring. A similar trend was identified in recent research that determined the methane profile of grazing lactating dairy cattle over a full grazing season through the use of the GreenFeed technology (B. Lahart, Teagasc, Cork, Ireland, per- sonal communication). This technology has also been deployed for dry dairy cattle that were housed and of- fered a grass silage diet (M. Kennedy, Teagasc, Cork, Ireland, personal communication). It is envisaged that this novel research, along with previously published data, will be used to develop a tier 3 methodology for enteric CH4 emissions from dairy cattle. Based on the sensitivity analysis, GWP could reduce to 0.91 and 0.71
kg of CO2-eq/kg of FPCM for the current and target dairy systems, respectively.

Allen et al. (2018), Cain et al. (2019), and Shine (2009) highlighted a serious limitation of the GWP metric, in that it does not adequately differentiate the different behaviors of short- and long-lived climate pollutants and their effects on global mean surface temperature. The ambiguous relationship between the ubiquitous GWP metric and temperature response is concerning, particularly in regards to the Paris Agree- ment, which has a clear temperature-oriented target. To address this, Cain et al. (2019) have presented a new metric, GWP*, or CO2 warming equivalent, which treats an increase in the emission rate of short-lived climate pollutants as a one-off pulse emission rather than as cumulative emissions, and provides a clear link between GHG emissions and temperature response (Lynch et al., 2020). This metric is not suitable for inclusion in farm-based carbon footprints, due to data requirements.
