7: <header>Acidification Potential </header>

As recommended in the European Commission (2018) Product Environmental Footprint guide, AP was calculated using country-specific characterization factors reported by Posch et al. (2008). This guide was developed to provide a consistent methodology and en- sure reproducibility and comparability of LCA results. The majority of previous studies, however, adopted alternative characterization factors (Huijbregts, 1999; Goedkoop et al., 2013). Research has reported AP of 6.9 and 11.9 kg of SO2-eq/ton of FPCM for a research pasture-based dairy system and a confinement-based system in Ireland, respectively (O'Brien et al., 2012). In contrast, Mu et al. (2017) reported an average AP of 26 (10.2-42.4) g of SO2-eq/kg of FPCM from 55 special- ized dairy systems across 6 European countries, 19 of which systems are in Ireland. Although variations be- tween studies are associated with differing management practices and inventory methodologies, disparity exists in characterization factors, where Mu et al. (2017) used ReCiPe (Goedkoop et al., 2013) whereas O'Brien et al. (2012) used Huijbregts (1999), the former placing greater AP on NH3 compared with the latter. Unlike Goedkoop et al. (2013) and Huijbregts (1999), the accu- mulated exceedance approach (Posch et al., 2008) used in the current study takes account of the dispersion of acidifying pollutants and the sensitivity of ecosystems receiving said acidifying pollutants.

Chobtang et al. (2016) reported AP of 13.5 E-03 to 16.4 E-03 molc H+-eq per kilogram of FPCM for dairy systems in the Waikato region, New Zealand. In con- trast, the current study reported AP of 6.8 E-03 and

4.7 E-03 molc H+-eq per kilogram of FPCM for the current and target systems, respectively. Difference is attributed to NH3 emission factors and characterization factors, with the current system using regional emis- sion factors and country-specific characterization fac- tors (Posch et al., 2008). Using the same methodology, Herron et al. (2021) calculated an AP of 5.76 to 6.21 mol H+-eq/ton of FPCM for 3 pasture-based research systems in Ireland. Both Herron et al. (2021) and the current study identify NH3 as the dominant acidifying pollutant from pasture-based systems, with manure management and synthetic fertilizer application be- ing key emission sources. The adoption of available technologies (i.e., low-emission slurry spreading and protected urea) and improving grassland and manure management in the target systems contributed to the mitigation of NH3 emissions and thus AP.
8: <header>Acidification Potential </header>

As recommended in the European Commission (2018) Product Environmental Footprint guide, AP was calculated using country-specific characterization factors reported by Posch et al. (2008). This guide was developed to provide a consistent methodology and en- sure reproducibility and comparability of LCA results. The majority of previous studies, however, adopted alternative characterization factors (Huijbregts, 1999; Goedkoop et al., 2013). Research has reported AP of 6.9 and 11.9 kg of SO2-eq/ton of FPCM for a research pasture-based dairy system and a confinement-based system in Ireland, respectively (O'Brien et al., 2012). In contrast, Mu et al. (2017) reported an average AP of 26 (10.2-42.4) g of SO2-eq/kg of FPCM from 55 special- ized dairy systems across 6 European countries, 19 of which systems are in Ireland. Although variations be- tween studies are associated with differing management practices and inventory methodologies, disparity exists in characterization factors, where Mu et al. (2017) used ReCiPe (Goedkoop et al., 2013) whereas O'Brien et al. (2012) used Huijbregts (1999), the former placing greater AP on NH3 compared with the latter. Unlike Goedkoop et al. (2013) and Huijbregts (1999), the accu- mulated exceedance approach (Posch et al., 2008) used in the current study takes account of the dispersion of acidifying pollutants and the sensitivity of ecosystems receiving said acidifying pollutants.

Chobtang et al. (2016) reported AP of 13.5 E-03 to 16.4 E-03 molc H+-eq per kilogram of FPCM for dairy systems in the Waikato region, New Zealand. In con- trast, the current study reported AP of 6.8 E-03 and

4.7 E-03 molc H+-eq per kilogram of FPCM for the current and target systems, respectively. Difference is attributed to NH3 emission factors and characterization factors, with the current system using regional emis- sion factors and country-specific characterization fac- tors (Posch et al., 2008). Using the same methodology, Herron et al. (2021) calculated an AP of 5.76 to 6.21 mol H+-eq/ton of FPCM for 3 pasture-based research systems in Ireland. Both Herron et al. (2021) and the current study identify NH3 as the dominant acidifying pollutant from pasture-based systems, with manure management and synthetic fertilizer application be- ing key emission sources. The adoption of available technologies (i.e., low-emission slurry spreading and protected urea) and improving grassland and manure management in the target systems contributed to the mitigation of NH3 emissions and thus AP.
9: <header>Eutrophication Potential </header>

Eutrophication was disaggregated into MEP and FEP using ReCiPe characterization factors (Huijbregts et al., 2016; European Commission, 2018). Previous research has used alternative characterization factors (Heijungs et al., 1992; Guinee et al., 2002) and thus can- not be directly compared; however, effects of manage- ment practices can be discussed. Previously, O'Brien et al. (2012) reported pasture-based dairy systems to have lower EP per kilogram of FPCM and per hectare than confinement dairy systems. The greater EP of the con- finement system was attributed to the indoor nature of the system emitting large quantities of NH3, and to the leaching of nutrients from feed and forage production. In an assessment of dairy systems in the Italian Alps, Battaglini et al. (2013) reported a positive relation- ship between EP per kilogram of FPCM and stock- ing rate. In contrast, the target system in the current study had lower MEP and FEP per kilogram of FPCM compared with the current system. In agreement with Battaglini et al. (2013), reduction in MEP is partially due to an increase in FPCM yield per cow and reduc- tion in purchased feed, as well as to the improvement in system efficiency and adoption of technologies. The sensitivity analysis indicates that improving grassland management to increase stocking rate will substantially increase MEP per hectare, along with other environ- mental indicators. Without the reduction in synthetic fertilizer through clover incorporation, the effect would be greater. In the target system, reduced concentrate supplementation counteracted the effect of improved grassland management, resulting in a marginal increase in MEP per hectare compared with the current system. Chobtang et al. (2016) reported MEP (2.7 g of N-eq/ kg of FPCM) from dairy systems in New Zealand

similar to those of the current study. In contrast, Mu et al. (2017) reported greater MEP (8.1 g of N-eq/kg of FPCM) for specialized dairy systems in Europe com- pared with the systems simulated in the current study. Differences in MEP are attributed to dissimilar man- agement practices (i.e., stocking rate, housing period, fertilizer rate, purchased feed), milk production, and LCA methodology (i.e., allocation method, character- ization factors) between studies. The majority of MEP occurred on farm (Table 5), with N fertilizer identified as a key source of MEP pollutants. This is in agreement with Chobtang et al. (2017), who also reported a posi- tive correlation of 0.65 between MEP per kilogram of FPCM and N fertilizer rate. Similar correlations were also reported for climate change (0.71) and AP (0.82). The current study reported lower FEP per kilogram of FPCM than Chobtang et al. (2016; 0.1 g/kg of FPCM) and Mu et al. (2017; 1.1 g/kg of FPCM). The low FEP is attributed to the current study importing less P onto the farm through inputs, P fertilizer application rate, and variation in life cycle inventory methodology. Using a similar methodology, Herron et al. (2021) reported lower FEP per kilogram of FPCM for research-based pasture systems, due to superior milk production and grassland management, and lower concentrate feed supplementation.

11: <header>Nonrenewable Energy Depletion </header>

Developments in synthetic N fertilizer production technology have reduced the energy embodied in syn- thetic N fertilizer (Table 1). Similarly, investment in renewable energy technologies has reduced the reliance of the national electricity grid on nonrenewable energy, with 37.5% of electricity generated by renewable energy sources in 2019. As a result, nonrenewable energy em- bodied in electricity was reduced from 7.78 MJ/kWh in 2005 to 4.4 MJ/kWh in 2019 (SEAI, 2020). These im- provements in energy efficiency are shown in this study when comparing the NRE consumption per kilogram of FPCM and per hectare of the current dairy system using the old and new LCA model (Table 5). O'Brien et al. (2012) reported NRE of 2.3 MJ/kg of FPCM for a pasture-based research dairy system, marginally lower than that of the current dairy system and greater than that of the target system. Concentrate feed was the dominant source of NRE in the current study; however, due to greater N fertilizer rate (260 kg of N/ ha) synthetic fertilizer production was the dominant source in the O'Brien et al. (2012) study. In contrast, the confinement dairy system had NRE of 3.9 MJ/ kg of FPCM, driven by concentrate feed production, electricity production, and fossil fuel use. The current study and that of O'Brien et al. (2012) highlight that

reducing reliance on synthetic N fertilizer and increas- ing the proportion of homegrown forage in the diet are 2 promising strategies to reduce NRE per kilogram of FPCM and per hectare from dairy systems. This is con- gruent with Chobtang et al. (2016), who report positive relationships between N fertilizer rate and concentrate feed supplementation across a range of environmental impact categories. Within this study, 2 systems were simulated, one be- ing representative of an average spring-calving pasture- based system, and the other based on high animal and grassland performance currently being achieved by the most efficient dairy farms. The lack of assessments of a nationally representative sample of dairy farms is a shortcoming of this study. The objective, however, was to conduct an LCA of the current average dairy system based on national statistics, using the previous and updated versions of the Moorepark LCA model, and to calculate the environmental impact of a target system reflective of the best technology today. This study has identified that readily available proven technologies (fertilizer, genetics, grassland management, manure management) will reduce the environmental impact per kilogram of FPCM. New research on developing country-specific emission factors and developing new mitigation strategies will be required to further im- prove the environmental sustainability of dairy systems beyond the target system.

13: <header>CONCLUSIONS </header>

Updating the LCA model using country-specific emis- sion factors and new data sources reduced the carbon footprint presented for the current Irish dairy farm by 10.4%. Reduction in on-farm emissions occurs through improvements in model accuracy and not improvements in production systems. Therefore, the reduction cannot be credited against any GHG reduction targets. The target dairy system was reported to have lower emis- sions intensities than the current dairy system across environmental impact categories investigated. Moving the current system toward the target system at farm level therefore has potential to reduce the environmen- tal impact of milk production. The reduction in envi- ronmental impact per kilogram of milk is attributed to the adoption of new technologies and improvements in system efficiency. However, we must recognize that, for some impact categories, the reduction in emissions intensities were offset by increases in productivity per hectare. The sensitivity analysis highlights the mitiga- tion potential of an average spring-calving pasture- based system adopting readily available technologies independently, and allows advisors and consultants to
choose technologies based on their impacts for indi- vidual farmers.

17: <header>Computing DMI </header>

Monthly feed intakes were calculated based on the total net energy (NE) requirements for milk produc- tion, maintenance, activity, pregnancy, and body weight change, and the NE value per kilogram of DM [unité fourragère lait (UFL)/kg of DM] of each feed in a given month (O'Mara, 1996). The net energy requirement is measured using the feed unit for lactation (UFL), where 1 UFL = 7.11 MJ of NE (Jarrige, 1989). Net energy provided by concentrate feed offered to cattle is calculated using the specified concentrate feeding rates for each month and the net energy content of the concentrate feed (UFL/kg of DM). The remaining total NE requirement is provided by forage per month. The proportion of NE provided by fresh forage is cal- culated based on the proportion of time spent grazing. Dry matter intakes are calculated using the NE value per kilogram of DM (UFL/kg of DM) of each feed in a given month, as follows.
Maintenance and activity

where BW = cow body weight (kg), Grazing = propor- tion of day grazing, AAG = 20% activity increase for grazing activity, Housing = proportion of day housed, and AAH = 10% increase for indoor cow activity.
Milk production = [(0.054 x Fat%) + (0.031 Prot%) + (0.028 x Lact%) 0.015] Milk,
where Fat% = milk fat percentage, Prot% = milk pro- tein percentage, Lact% = milk lactose percentage, and Milk = milk yield per day (kg).
Pregnancy = 153 UFL/cow,
distributed across the last 3 mo of pregnancy, increas- ing from 0.9 UFL/d to 1.6 UFL/d and to 2.6 UFL/d before calving.
Body weight changes are calculated as follows:
BW increase = 4.5 UFL/kg LWG DLWG; BW decrease = -3.5 UFL/kg LWG DLWG, where LWG = live weight gain, and DLWG = daily live weight gain (kg/d).
